title: Quiz: Molecular Properties from HF
description: Test your understanding of property calculation from HF wavefunctions

questions.question: How are one-electron properties calculated from the HF wavefunction?

options.text: ⟨Ô⟩ = Tr(P @ O) where P is the density matrix
⟨Ô⟩ = Tr(F @ O) where F is the Fock matrix
⟨Ô⟩ = Tr(S @ O) where S is overlap

questions.explanation: One-electron properties are calculated as ⟨Ô⟩ = Tr(P @ O) = Σμν Pμν Oνμ, where P is the density matrix and O is the property operator matrix in AO basis.

questions.question: What does Mulliken population analysis calculate?
questions.explanation: Mulliken analysis partitions the electron density among atoms based on the overlap-weighted density matrix, giving partial charges on each atom.

questions.question: What does an imaginary vibrational frequency indicate?

options.text: A transition state or saddle point

questions.explanation: Imaginary frequencies (negative eigenvalues of the Hessian) indicate curvature in the wrong direction—a saddle point or transition state, not a minimum.

questions.question: What is the typical accuracy of HF for equilibrium bond lengths?
questions.explanation: HF typically predicts bond lengths within 0.01-0.02 Å of experiment, though bonds tend to be slightly too short due to missing correlation.

questions.question: HF vibrational frequencies are typically 5-10% higher than experimental values.
questions.explanation: True. HF frequencies are systematically too high, requiring scale factors (typically 0.89-0.93) for comparison with experiment.

questions.question: What is molecular polarizability?

options.text: The response of the dipole moment to an electric field

questions.explanation: Polarizability αᵢⱼ = ∂μᵢ/∂Eⱼ measures how the dipole moment changes in response to an applied electric field—the ease of electron density distortion.

questions.question: Which property does HF predict LEAST accurately?
questions.explanation: Atomization energies (and reaction energies) are poorly predicted by HF because they depend critically on electron correlation, which HF misses. Errors of 10-20% are common.

questions.question: Which population analysis methods are generally more reliable than Mulliken? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: NPA, Hirshfeld, and Bader are more robust than Mulliken. NPA is basis-set stable, Hirshfeld always gives positive populations, and Bader is topologically rigorous. There's no 'Core Hamiltonian analysis' for charges.
